title: Jeremiah 51
subtitle: Oracle Against Babylon (Part 2)

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Fall of Jerusalem

sections.header: Verses 1-33 -- "The LORD Has Stirred Up the Kings of the Medes"

heb.paragraph: "I will stir up a destroying wind against Babylon and against the people of Leb Kamai" (v.1). Leb Kamai is an atbash cipher for Kasdim (Chaldeans) -- the same encoding technique used for "Sheshak" (= Babel) in 25:26. The coded names may reflect the danger of openly proclaiming Babylon's doom while living under Babylonian rule.

refs.note: The handwriting on the wall: "Mene, Mene, Tekel, Parsin" -- Babylon weighed and found wanting.
"The woman held a golden cup in her hand, filled with abominable things" -- directly echoing Jeremiah 51:7.

notes.note: "Israel and Judah have not been forsaken by their God" -- even in exile, even under Babylon's power, the covenant holds. God has not abandoned his people.
"Flee from Babylon! Save yourselves!" The command to leave echoes the call to Lot to leave Sodom. Babylon is the new Sodom -- incurable and doomed.
"The LORD has stirred up the kings of the Medes." The Medes are named as Babylon's destroyer -- a prophecy fulfilled when Cyrus (half-Mede, half-Persian) conquered the city in 539 BC.

The creation hymn: God made the earth by his power, established the world by his wisdom. The same God who controls creation controls history. Idols are nothing; "the Portion of Jacob is not like these."

Calvin develops the golden cup: "Babylon was God's instrument -- the cup of wrath nations were forced to drink (25:15). But the cup-bearer who served God's judgment is now judged for the cruelty with which she executed it.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The atbash cipher Leb Qamai encodes Kasdim (Chaldeans) by substituting each Hebrew letter with its reverse-alphabet equivalent -- the same technique used for Sheshak/Babel.
The "kings of the Medes" represent the Median component of the Medo-Persian empire that conquered Babylon under Cyrus in 539 BC.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies the creation hymn (vv.15-19) as "the theological center of the oracle." Judgment on Babylon is grounded in creation -- the God who made everything has authority to unmake any empire. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads "flee from Babylon" as the programmatic call of the entire exile tradition. The command extends beyond geography to theology: leave the imperial system, its values, its gods. Second Isaiah (Isa 48:20) and Revelation (18:4) repeat the same call.

hist.context: Chapter 51 continues and intensifies the Babylon oracle. The theological argument deepens: Babylon is guilty because she sinned against the Holy One of Israel (v.5), used gold cup of judgment to intoxicate the nations (v.7), and refused to be healed (v.9). The Medes are named as the instrument of Babylon's destruction (v.11) -- fulfilled when the Medo-Persian coalition under Cyrus conquered Babylon in 539 BC. The creation hymn (vv.15-19) -- identical to 10:12-16 -- anchors the judgment in creation theology.

sections.header: Verses 34-64 -- "Repay Babylon for What She Has Done"

heb.paragraph: Jeremiah writes all the words of Babylon's doom on a scroll (sepher, v.60) and gives it to Seraiah to read aloud in Babylon, then tie a stone to it and throw it into the Euphrates: "So will Babylon sink to rise no more" (v.64). This is the final sign-act of the book -- the written word of judgment sinking into the river as the city it condemns will sink into history.

refs.note: "A mighty angel picked up a boulder the size of a large millstone and threw it into the sea: 'With such violence the great city of Babylon will be thrown down.'" Revelation directly imitates Jeremiah's sinking scroll.
Isaiah names Cyrus as the LORD's anointed who will conquer Babylon -- the parallel prophecy fulfilled in 539 BC.

notes.note: Zion speaks: "Nebuchadnezzar has devoured us ... let the violence done to our flesh be on Babylon." The victim's voice demands justice, and God grants it.
"Sheshak" (atbash for Babylon) taken, the sea covers her, the land becomes a desolation. The imagery is of cosmic reversal -- creation undone, as in 4:23-26.
The scroll sign-act: Seraiah reads the oracle in Babylon itself, ties a stone to the scroll, and throws it into the Euphrates. "So will Babylon sink." The physical act enacts the prophetic word.
"The words of Jeremiah end here." The editorial colophon marks the close of the prophetic collection. Chapter 52 is a historical appendix.

Calvin reads the sinking scroll as a final statement about the power of God's word: "The scroll sinks but the word rises. What is written and sealed cannot be undone by any human power. Babylon sinks; the truth floats.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Seraiah son of Neriah is Baruch's brother (cf. 32:12, where Baruch is "son of Neriah"). Both brothers serve Jeremiah -- Baruch as scribe, Seraiah as courier.
"The words of Jeremiah end here" ('ad-henna dibre yirmeyahu) is a scribal colophon marking the boundary between the prophetic oracles and the historical appendix of chapter 52.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom calls the sinking scroll "the final sign-act and the perfect closure." The book that began with the word of the LORD (1:4) ends with the word sinking into the Euphrates -- the word goes into the deep but cannot be silenced. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the sinking scroll as "the ultimate performative act." The word does not merely describe Babylon's fall -- it enacts it. The scroll in the river is Babylon in history: going down, not to rise again. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: The oracle concludes with Zion's vindication (vv.34-40), cosmic imagery of Babylon's fall (vv.41-48), and the scroll sign-act (vv.59-64). Seraiah, Baruch's brother (v.59; cf. 32:12), carries the scroll to Babylon in the fourth year of Zedekiah (594 BC) -- the same year as the yoke episode (ch. 27). The sinking scroll is the last prophetic performance: the word of judgment literally goes down, taking Babylon with it. Verse 64b -- "The words of Jeremiah end here" -- is the colophon marking the end of the prophetic collection.

rows.label: vv.6-10
rows.text: Flee from Babylon: The Golden Cup

rows.label: vv.49-58
rows.text: The City of Chaos: Walls Demolished

rows.label: vv.59-64
rows.text: The Sinking Scroll: The Words End Here

lit.note: The climactic oracle concludes with the sinking scroll -- the word of judgment performed as prophetic sign-act. "The words of Jeremiah end here" closes the prophetic collection.

themes.note: Judgment and Sovereignty both at 10 -- the supreme oracle against the supreme empire. Prophecy at 9: the sinking scroll is the final prophetic act.

Leb Kamai is an atbash cipher for Kasdim (Chaldeans) -- the same encoding technique used for "Sheshak" (= Babel) in 25:26. The coded names may reflect the danger of openly proclaiming Babylon's doom while living under Babylonian rule.

Jeremiah writes all the words of Babylon's doom on a scroll (sepher, v.60) and gives it to Seraiah to read aloud in Babylon, then tie a stone to it and throw it into the Euphrates: "So will Babylon sink to rise no more" (v.64). This is the final sign-act of the book -- the written word of judgment sinking into the river as the city it condemns will sink into history.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Oracle Against Babylon (Part 2)

translations.text: I will send foreigners to Babylon to winnow her and to devastate her land; they will oppose her on every side in the day of her disaster.
and I will send to Babylon winnowers, and they shall winnow her, and they shall empty her land, when they come against her from every side on the day of trouble.
“Flee from Babylon! Run for your lives! Do not be destroyed because of her sins. It is time for the Lord’s vengeance; he will repay her what she deserves.
“Flee from the midst of Babylon; let every one save his life! Be not cut off in her punishment, for this is the time of the LORD’s vengeance, the repayment he is rendering her.

thread.text: The handwriting on the wall: "Mene, Mene, Tekel, Parsin" -- Babylon weighed and found wanting.
"The woman held a golden cup in her hand, filled with abominable things" -- directly echoing Jeremiah 51:7.

"A mighty angel picked up a boulder the size of a large millstone and threw it into the sea: 'With such violence the great city of Babylon will be thrown down.'" Revelation directly imitates Jeremiah's sinking scroll.
Isaiah names Cyrus as the LORD's anointed who will conquer Babylon -- the parallel prophecy fulfilled in 539 BC.

coaching.tip: 'Babylon was a gold cup in the LORD's hand' (v. 7) — even at its worst, Babylon served God's purposes. But the cup that made the nations drunk will be shattered. Revelation 17–18 develops this imagery extensively: 'Babylon the Great' holds a golden cup full of abominations. Jeremiah's metaphor travels intact from the sixth century BC to the first century AD. The prophetic vocabulary proves more durable than the empires it describes.

Jeremiah writes the Babylon oracle on a scroll, ties it to a stone, and has Seraiah throw it into the Euphrates: 'So will Babylon sink to rise no more' (v. 64). The sign-act closes the prophetic career: the written word, physically sunk, performs the judgment it describes. Revelation 18:21 echoes this exactly — an angel hurls a stone into the sea saying 'Babylon will be thrown down.' Jeremiah writes the script; Revelation stages the finale.

prayer_prompt: As the scroll of Babylon's doom is tied to a stone and sunk in the Euphrates, consider asking God to assure you that every oppressive system will one day sink — and that His word guarantees it.
